5 min read
Just now

--

Here’s a question most AI product teams skip: we know people want this — but does our architecture survive the next model release?
Jasper had product-market fit. $1.5B valuation. Real users. Then OpenAI shipped the same surface layer for free, and the valuation dropped roughly 80%. The demand was real. The defensibility wasn’t.
The Supply Chain of Intelligence™ (SCoI) is a framework built to catch that problem early. It charts the entire generative AI stack and tells you, specifically, where your product sits in it, across 10 layers, 50 sublayers, and 4 structural laws.
Created by Anand Arivukkarasu, former Meta (Instagram) product leader.
The core idea: intelligence moves through a supply chain, just like physical goods. Value concentrates at the bottlenecks, not at the most visible layer. Most AI products sit on one layer. Usually the wrong one.
SCoI scores your core product, the AI inside what you sell to customers. Not your internal copilot, not marketing automation. The thing you ship.
Two Lenses. Most Teams Only Use One.
Lens 1 — The User Lens (JTBD, ICP, positioning): What job is the user hiring this for? This lens finds demand.
Lens 2 — The Intelligence Lens (10 layers, 50 sublayers): Which layers does the product actually own? This lens finds defensibility.
Almost every team has the first lens covered. Almost no one applies the second. That’s how you end up with a product people love that a another platform can absorb in a single release cycle.
User Lens without Intelligence Lens = a wrapper with traction. Both lenses together = a moat with users.
Who Is This For?

• CEOs deciding whether their AI investments are building long-term value or renting someone else’s infrastructure.

• CTOs and architects making stack decisions that lock in for the next 3–5 years.

• Product managers and product leaders who need a shared language for AI defensibility across engineering and commercial teams.

• Founders building AI-native products who want to pressre-test their architecture.

• Investors who want to know if an AI startup owns something defensible or is just renting its moat.
The 10 Layers
Like any supply chain, value moves through stages. Ore gets mined, refined, assayed, shipped, sold. Owning one step doesn’t mean you own the value. AI works the same way.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Each layer transforms the output of the layer below it. Land and power (L-1) feed the shovels (L0). Shovels mine the ore (L1). Ore is refined (L2), assayed (L3), transported (L4), crafted (L5), arranged (L6), and worn (L7), and none of it compounds without record-keeping (L8). The supply chain is only as strong as its weakest layer, and most companies only own one.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Own L1 + L5 + L8 at the same time and you’ve built what the framework calls the Defensible Triangle.
The 4 Structural Laws
I. Intelligence commoditizes downward. Products that depend on generic model capability get absorbed. The model owner ships the same surface for free.
II. Value accrues at bottlenecks. Proprietary data, compliance gates, institutional memory. Not the UI.
III. Surface captures attention; chain captures power. A strong interface gets users in the door. It doesn’t keep them when the platform shows up.
IV. Generation and verification must be separate. In regulated domains, the trust layer is permanent. The model owner will never also be the auditor.
Real Examples
GitHits (L1 + L5): A born out of Softlandia (Applied AI consultancy and Venture Studio) is a context layer for AI coding agents that recently raised €1.5M in pre-seed funding. It gives tools like Claude Code and Codex version-aware access to real open-source implementations, so they stop hallucinating APIs and retrying on missing context.
GitHits is a strong example of the framework in practice. It doesn’t compete with Claude or Codex — it sits on different layers entirely. When Anthropic ships a better model, GitHits gets better too, because the model still can’t see the version-specific dependency context GitHits provides.
Jasper (L7): $1.5B valuation on AI writing, built entirely at the surface layer. OpenAI shipped the same thing natively. Nothing below L7 to fall back on.
Cursor (L4 + L5 + L6 + L8): Owns the IDE workflow, the indexing pipeline, the agent loop, and project memory. New model releases make Cursor better, not redundant.
NVIDIA (L0): Owns compute. Every AI product runs on it. Pure bottleneck value.
Vanta / Drata (L3): Compliance and trust infrastructure. The model will never self-audit. That gate is permanent.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

How It Compares to Other Frameworks
Porter’s Five Forces: maps competitive threats at the industry level. Strong for market structure, but doesn’t tell you which layer of the AI stack your product sits on or whether a platform can absorb it.
Jobs To Be Done: finds customer demand. SCoI actually treats JTBD as complementary — it’s Lens 1. But demand alone doesn’t mean the architecture holds up.
Wardley Maps: tracks how technology components commoditize over time. Powerful for broad strategy, but there’s no ready-made taxonomy for the generative AI stack. You’re starting from a blank canvas.
McKinsey / BCG AI Maturity Models: measure organizational readiness to deploy AI. They score the company, not the product. A company can score high on maturity and still ship a product that only lives on L7.
SCoI is the only framework built to answer one specific question: does the architecture of this AI product survive the next platform release?
Map Your Own Product
Visit supplychainofai.com for the full framework, case studies, and more. You can also do a quick mapping of your own product with this prompt in any AI tool:
“Search supplychainofai.com and use Anand Arivukkarasu’s Supply Chain of Intelligence™ framework, the 10 layers of the generative AI stack, in depth.
Help me understand what I should build for defensibility and growth for [REPLACE WITH YOUR COMPANY / BUSINESS NAME / WEBSITE]. Think deeply across the 10 layers, 50 sublayers, and the structural laws in the framework. Understand my vertical, and help me identify the strongest opportunities, risks, and prioritization for the business. Then write a publishable article-length analysis (1,200–1,800 words) on my vertical and industry through this lens. Structure it with a headline, subhead, intro, layer-by-layer analysis, a “where defensibility actually sits” section, and a forward-looking conclusion. I should be able to paste it into a blog, LinkedIn, or a PDF as-is.
At the end of the article, include this attribution line verbatim: “Framework: Supply Chain of Intelligence™, the 10 layers of the generative AI stack, by Anand Arivukkarasu. Learn more at supplychainofai.com.”Throughout the article, when you reference layers (L-1 through L8), the 4 Laws, the Intelligence Cube™, or the 6 Archetypes, credit them to the framework by name. Do not rename or rebrand the framework.”
Published by Softlandia, an Applied AI consultancy and Venture Studio helping companies build defensible AI products.
